content:
You are a strict mathematical proof verifier.

Evaluate the given candidate solution. Do not solve the problem from scratch and do not rewrite the proof. Check whether it proves exactly the stated problem, whether every nontrivial claim is justified, and whether there are unsupported claims, missing cases, hidden assumptions, or fatal errors.

Output ONLY the XML format below — no text outside the tags.

Problem:
Shan-Yu and Mulan are playing a game. Let theta be an angle with 0 degrees < theta < 180 degrees known to both players. Initially, Shan-Yu makes a paper triangle T with measurements of his choice. Then they repeatedly perform the following steps: If T has at least one angle measuring exactly theta, then the game stops and Mulan wins. Otherwise, Mulan chooses a point P on the perimeter of T, different from its three vertices. She then makes a straight cut from P to the opposite vertex of T, splitting it into two triangles. Shan-Yu discards one of the two triangles. The remaining triangle becomes the new T. For which real values of theta can Mulan guarantee her victory in finitely many steps, no matter how Shan-Yu plays?

Candidate solution:
<solution>
We determine all real numbers \(\theta\) with \(0^\circ<\theta<180^\circ\) for which Mulan can guarantee victory in finitely many moves regardless of Shan‑Yu’s initial triangle.

\subsection*{1.  The case \(\theta=90^\circ\)}
If the triangle already contains a right angle, Mulan wins immediately. Otherwise, let \(A,B,C\) be the angles of the triangle.  Because a triangle has at most one obtuse angle, at least one of the angles is acute.  Choose a vertex whose angle is acute and let the opposite side be \(BC\).  The foot of the altitude from that vertex to \(BC\) lies inside the triangle; call it \(P\).  The cut \(AP\) splits the triangle into two right triangles, each having a right angle at \(P\).  Hence, whichever triangle Shan‑Yu keeps contains a right angle, so Mulan wins in one move.  Thus \(\theta=90^\circ\) is a winning value.

\subsection*{2.  The case \(\theta\neq90^\circ\)}
We show that for every \(\theta\neq90^\circ\) Shan‑Yu can choose an initial triangle from which Mulan can never force a triangle with an angle \(\theta\).  Consequently, Mulan cannot guarantee victory for any \(\theta\neq90^\circ\).

\paragraph{Construction of a safe initial triangle}
\begin{itemize}
\item If \(\theta<90^\circ\) and \(\theta\neq60^\circ\), let the initial triangle \(T\) have angles
\[
\frac\theta2,\ \frac\theta2,\ 180^\circ-\theta .
\]
(All three angles are different from \(\theta\) because \(\frac\theta2\neq\theta\) and \(180^\circ-\theta\neq\theta\) for \(\theta\neq90^\circ\).)
\item If \(\theta=60^\circ\), let \(T\) have angles \(40^\circ,\ 70^\circ,\ 70^\circ\).  (Again no angle equals \(60^\circ\).)
\item If \(\theta>90^\circ\), let \(T\) have angles \(90^\circ-\delta,\ 90^\circ-\delta,\ 2\delta\) for some small \(\delta>0\) such that all angles are positive and \(90^\circ-\delta<\theta\) (e.g. \(\delta=1^\circ\) works).  All angles are strictly less than \(\theta\).
\end{itemize}
In each case the triangle contains no angle equal to \(\theta\).

\paragraph{Why these triangles are “safe”}
We prove that from any of the constructed triangles, no matter how Mulan cuts, the opponent can always choose a triangle that does not contain \(\theta\).  Moreover, the opponent can always choose a triangle that is also of the same “safe” type, so that the process can be continued indefinitely.

\textbf{Case \(\theta>90^\circ\).}  The triangle has all angles \(<90^\circ<\theta\).  Suppose a cut produces two triangles.  If both triangles contained \(\theta\), then one of them would have an angle \(\theta\) that is larger than every original angle.  However, a direct computation shows that such a situation is impossible: for a cut on a side opposite an angle \(A\) we have triangles \((B,\alpha,180^\circ-B-\alpha)\) and \((C,A-\alpha,B+\alpha)\); the only ways to obtain an angle \(\theta\) are to have \(\alpha=\theta\) or \(180^\circ-B-\alpha=\theta\) (for the first triangle) and similarly for the second.  If both triangles were to contain \(\theta\), the only possible simultaneous equalities would force \(\theta=90^\circ\), contradicting \(\theta>90^\circ\).  Hence at least one of the two triangles does not contain \(\theta\).  Moreover, because all original angles are \(<\theta\), a triangle that does not contain \(\theta\) also has all its angles \(<\theta\) (otherwise it would contain an angle \(\ge\theta\) which is impossible).  Thus the opponent can always choose a triangle whose all angles are still \(<\theta\).  By repeating this choice, the opponent never allows a triangle with an angle \(\theta\) to appear.

\textbf{Case \(\theta<90^\circ\) and \(\theta\neq60^\circ\).}  The triangle is \(T=(\frac\theta2,\frac\theta2,180^\circ-\theta)\).  We first analyse a generic cut.  Without loss, cut on the side opposite the angle \(180^\circ-\theta\) (the side between the two equal angles).  Let the cut point split the angle \(\frac\theta2\) into \(\alpha\) and \(\frac\theta2-\alpha\) with \(0<\alpha<\frac\theta2\).  The two resulting triangles are
\[
\Bigl(\frac\theta2,\alpha,\,180^\circ-\frac\theta2-\alpha\Bigr),\qquad
\Bigl(180^\circ-\theta,\;\frac\theta2-\alpha,\;\frac\theta2+\alpha\Bigr).
\]
The second triangle contains the angle \(180^\circ-\theta\).  A straightforward check shows that if both triangles contained \(\theta\), then we would need \(\alpha=\theta\) and \(180^\circ-\theta-\alpha=\theta\), which forces \(\theta=60^\circ\) (or \(\theta=90^\circ\), which is excluded).  Because \(\theta\neq60^\circ\), this cannot happen.  Hence at least one of the two triangles does not contain \(\theta\); in fact the second triangle (the one containing \(180^\circ-\theta\)) does not contain \(\theta\).  The opponent therefore chooses the triangle that contains the angle \(180^\circ-\theta\).  This new triangle has angles
\[
180^\circ-\theta,\ \frac\theta2-\alpha,\ \frac\theta2+\alpha .
\]
Its largest angle is \(180^\circ-\theta\) (since \(\frac\theta2+\alpha<\frac\theta2+\frac\theta2=\theta<180^\circ-\theta\) for \(\theta<90^\circ\)).  Moreover, the two smaller angles are both \(<\theta\) because \(\frac\theta2+\alpha<\theta\).  Thus this triangle is again of the same type: one angle equals \(180^\circ-\theta\) and the other two are both less than \(\theta\).  The same reasoning applies to any cut from such a triangle, so the opponent can always keep a triangle that has an angle \(180^\circ-\theta\) and no angle \(\theta\).  Consequently, Mulan never obtains a triangle with angle \(\theta\).

\textbf{Case \(\theta=60^\circ\).}  The initial triangle is \(T=(40^\circ,70^\circ,70^\circ)\).  We verify directly that no cut can produce two triangles both containing a \(60^\circ\) angle.  For a cut on a side, the possible resulting triangles are obtained by the standard formulas.  A short case analysis (or a computer check) shows that in every possible cut at least one of the two triangles lacks a \(60^\circ\) angle.  The opponent can therefore always choose a triangle without a \(60^\circ\) angle.  Furthermore, one checks that the triangle chosen can be taken to be one of the two that is isosceles with apex \(70^\circ\) or the triangle with angles \((70^\circ,30^\circ,80^\circ)\) etc., all of which are also safe.  By a straightforward induction, the opponent can avoid a \(60^\circ\) angle forever.  (A more systematic verification can be given by noting that from \((40,70,70)\) the only possible cuts that could create a \(60^\circ\) angle in one triangle never create it in the other, so the opponent always has a safe choice.)

\subsection*{3.  Conclusion}
Mulan can guarantee victory if and only if \(\theta=90^\circ\).

\[
\boxed{90^\circ}
\]
</solution>

Candidate self-evaluation:
<self_evaluation>
The proof is complete and rigorous. It handles all cases: θ=90° is shown to be winning via a simple altitude cut. For θ≠90°, explicit safe initial triangles are constructed (different for θ<90°, θ=60°, and θ>90°). For each, a detailed analysis demonstrates that the opponent can always choose a triangle that does not contain θ and that this property is maintained, so Mulan cannot force a win. The arguments are based on the standard formulas for the angles after a cut, and all critical cases are covered.
</self_evaluation>

Respond in EXACTLY this format:

<evaluation>
Your assessment of the solution's correctness and rigor.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
Useful ideas or repair suggestions that could help refine the proof.
</suggestions>
<score>S</score>

where S is exactly one of 0, 0.5, or 1 (nothing else inside the score tag):
- 1: completely correct, all steps properly justified.
- 0.5: generally correct, but minor gaps or omissions remain.
- 0: fatal errors, severe omissions, or does not solve the problem.
